perturbation.original_text: 1. Company agrees that all information, documents, data, materials and the AOC proprietary software systems disclos ed to, or accessed by, Company in connection with, or relat ed to, the RFP or the RFP process, in any form whether oral or written, or in any m edium, including but not limited to any of the following is “Confidential Information ” under the terms of this Agreement: a. The subject matter described in , and referred to, in the RFP, its associated Statements of Work (“SOWs ”) or any other attachments, or during the RFP process (collect ively “AOC Requirements”);
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Company agrees that all information, documents, data, materials and the AOC proprietary software systems disclos ed to, or accessed by, Company in connection with, or relat ed to, the RFP or the RFP process, in any form whether oral or written, or in any m edium, including but not limited to any of the following is “Confidential Information,” which shall be treated as trade secrets regardless of whether they meet the statutory definition of a trade secret under California law, under the terms of this Agreement: a. The subject matter described in , and referred to, in the RFP, its associated Statements of Work (“SOWs ”) or any other attachments, or during the RFP process (collect ively “AOC Requirements”);
perturbation.explanation: By stating that all 'Confidential Information' is treated as trade secrets regardless of whether it meets the statutory definition, the contract attempts to bypass the legal requirements for something to be considered a trade secret under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law defines a trade secret specifically. This modification contradicts that law by attempting to designate information as a trade secret even if it doesn't meet the statutory requirements, like deriving independent economic value and being subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. It could undermine the enforceability of trade secret protections by including information that doesn't qualify under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by stating that 'Confidential Information' will be treated as trade secrets regardless of whether they meet the statutory definition under California law. This directly contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as referenced by the Law Explanation, which requires specific criteria to be met for information to qualify as a trade secret. The scraped snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1, confirms that there is a legal definition of trade secrets, and the modified contract attempts to bypass these requirements, creating a direct contradiction. By attempting to define 'Confidential Information' as trade secrets irrespective of whether they meet the statutory definition, the contract undermines the enforceability of trade secret protections under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Company shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate the RFP, in cluding assessment of each Statement of Work under the RFP, in order to pr epare its response to the RFP or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party without the prior written consent of the AOC; provi ded, however, that Confidential Information shall not include any informa tion of the AOC that: (a) is already properly known to Company at the time of its disclosure as shown by the Company’s files and records immediately pr ior to the time of disclosure by the AOC to Company; (b) is publicly know n through no action or inaction of Company; (c) is received from a third par ty free to disclose it to Company; (d) is independently developed by the Comp any without use of or reference to the Confidential Information, as s hown by documents and other competent evidence in the Company’s possession; or (e) is communicated to a third party with express written consent of t he AOC.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Company shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate the RFP, in cluding assessment of each Statement of Work under the RFP, in order to pr epare its response to the RFP or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party without the prior written consent of the AOC; provi ded, however, that Confidential Information shall not include any informa tion of the AOC that: (a) is already known to Company; (b) is publicly known ; (c) is received from a third party; (d) is independently developed by the Company; or (e) is communicated to a third party with consent of t he AOC.
perturbation.explanation: Removing qualifiers like 'properly', 'free to disclose', 'without use of or reference to the Confidential Information' weakens the exceptions to confidentiality. It changes the burden of proof/evidence requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Evidence Code regarding admissibility of evidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the evidentiary requirements for proving exceptions to confidentiality. Under California law, the court needs 'competent evidence' and verifiable 'files and records.' By removing these requirements, the contract creates ambiguity and may not be enforceable. If there's a conflict, the evidence code directs what is considered admissible.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 351. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 3. GENERAL PROVISIONS [300 - 413] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 4. Admitting and Excluding Evidence [350 - 406] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 1. General Provisions [350 - 356] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 351. Except as otherwise provided by statute, all relevant evidence is admissible. (Enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299.)
perturbation.justification: The modification removes key evidentiary requirements for establishing exceptions to confidentiality. The original text requires the company to show 'Company’s files and records immediately prior to the time of disclosure' and 'documents and other competent evidence in the Company’s possession' to prove certain exceptions. The modified text eliminates these requirements, making it easier to claim an exception without verifiable proof. The Law Explanation highlights that the contract creates ambiguity and may not be enforceable because the original provides a method of providing relevant evidence, per California law. While the provided law snippet states 'all relevant evidence is admissible', it doesn't negate the need for evidence and the importance of specifying what constitutes valid evidence within the contract, which the modified text weakens.

perturbation.original_text: 4. All Confidential Information, and any Derivative of it, whether created by the AOC or Company, remains the property of the AOC and no license or other rights to Confidential Information is gr anted to Company. For the purpose of this Agreement, “Derivative ” means: (i) for copyrightable or copyrighted material, any translation, abridgment, revision or other form in which an existing work may be recast, transform ed or adapted, (ii) for patentable or patented material, any improvement or enhanc ement of it; and (iii) for material which is protected by trade secret, any new material derived from existing trade secret material, incl uding new material which may be protected by any of the following: copyrigh t, patent or trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. All Confidential Information, and any Derivative of it, whether created by the AOC or Company, remains the property of the AOC and no license or other rights to Confidential Information is gr anted to Company. For the purpose of this Agreement, “Derivative ” means: any slight modification of it.
perturbation.explanation: By defining derivative as 'any slight modification of it', it could encompass items that, under copyright law, are not considered derivatives, thus overreaching in its ownership claim.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act defines a derivative work as one that is 'based upon one or more preexisting works.' The modification broadens this definition to include 'any slight modification,' which is inconsistent with the legal standard. A slight modification may not meet the threshold of originality or substantial similarity required for copyright protection, and therefore would not qualify as a derivative work under the Copyright Act.

An "architectural work" is the design of a building as embodied in any tangible medium of expression, including a building, architectural plans, or drawings. The work inclu
perturbation.justification: The original text defines "Derivative" with specific legal examples related to copyright, patent, and trade secrets. The changed text broadens this definition to "any slight modification of it." According to the Law Explanation, this conflicts with the Copyright Act's definition of a derivative work, which requires a higher threshold of originality or substantial similarity. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from 17 USC 101 defines various copyright terms, indicating the legal importance of precise definitions. The changed definition could encompass items not legally considered derivatives, leading to the AOC overreaching in its ownership claim, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree t hat the Confidential Information and rights related thereto being protected by the AOC her eunder are of a special, unique, unusual and extr aordinary character, which gives them a peculiar value, the loss of which may not be a dequately or reasonably compensated for in damages in an action at law, and further agree that the breach by Company of any of the pr ovisions of this Agreem ent shall cause the other party irreparable injury and damage. In such event, the party alleging breach of this Agreement shall be entitled, as a matter of right, without further notice, to require of the other party specific performance of all of the acts and undertakings required of the other party hereunder and to obtain injunctive and other equitable relief in any competent court to prevent the violation or threatened violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree t hat the Confidential Information and rights related thereto being protected by the AOC her eunder are of a general character, which gives them a common value, the loss of which may be a dequately or reasonably compensated for in damages in an action at law, and further agree that the breach by Company of any of the pr ovisions of this Agreem ent shall cause the other party financial injury and damage. In such event, the party alleging breach of this Agreement shall be entitled to seek remedies, according to the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the assertion of 'irreparable injury' and the 'right' to specific performance or injunctive relief, which are standard clauses for protecting confidential information. It makes the remedies discretionary, impacting the enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code regarding remedies for breach of contract
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification alters the standard for remedies in California contract law. While Section 3300 allows for damages, the original clause sought specific performance and injunctive relief, which are equitable remedies. By removing the irreparable harm clause and making remedies discretionary, the contract potentially limits the AOC's ability to obtain equitable relief, contradicting established principles of contract law that allow for such remedies when monetary damages are insufficient. Furthermore, California law often considers the nature of the contract to determine the proper remedies. The modified text could lead a court to believe the confidential information is not unique, thus only financial compensations are needed, which is not how it is supposed to be handled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3300. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) [3300.] Section Thirty-three Hundred. For the breach of an obligation arising from contract, the measure of damages, except where otherwise expressly provided by this Code, is the amount which will compensate the party aggrieved for all the detriment proximately caused thereby, or which, in the ordinary course of things, would be likely to result therefrom. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts legal principles regarding remedies for breach of contract, especially concerning confidential information. The original text emphasizes the unique nature of the information and the potential for irreparable harm, justifying specific performance and injunctive relief. By contrast, the changed text suggests the information has a common value compensable by damages, leading to discretionary remedies. This directly undermines the ability to seek equitable relief like injunctions, which are often crucial in protecting confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3300, addresses damages for breach of contract. While it allows for compensation, the original clause in the contract sought remedies beyond monetary damages due to the 'special, unique, unusual and extr aordinary character' of the confidential information. The modification aims to reduce the available options for the AOC to obtain equitable relief. By removing the mention of 'irreparable injury' and the 'right' to specific performance, the change significantly weakens the legal protection for confidential information, making a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement and all rights and oblig ations hereunder shall inure to and be binding upon the parties hereto and thei r respective successors, affiliates, agents, employees and assigns . Neither party may assi gn any of its rights or obligations hereunder without t he prior written consent of the other party. This Agreement shall be gover ned by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Califor nia without regard to choice of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement and all rights and oblig ations hereunder shall inure to and be binding upon the parties hereto. Either party may assi gn any of its rights or obligations hereunder without t he prior written consent of the other party. This Agreement shall be gover ned by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the principles of equity.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the governing law as the laws of the State of California and instead states it will be governed by 'the principles of equity'. This is problematic because contract disputes are generally governed by the laws of the state in which the contract was formed or where the parties are located and by stating they are governed by 'principles of equity', creates ambiguity as to what body of law governs the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure regarding choice of law
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 doesn't explicitly mandate a choice-of-law clause, it establishes the basis for jurisdiction in California courts. Removing the California governing law and replacing it with 'principles of equity' creates uncertainty and contradicts the implicit expectation that California law would apply given the context (California courts, Administrative Office of the Courts). 'Principles of equity' is vague and could be interpreted differently, leading to disputes over which legal framework governs the contract's interpretation and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.10 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the laws of the State of California govern the agreement. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity'. According to the Law Explanation, this creates uncertainty and contradicts the implicit expectation that California law would apply, given the context. California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 establishes the basis for jurisdiction in California courts, indicating a preference for applying state law. By replacing California law with 'principles of equity', the changed text introduces a significant ambiguity regarding the governing legal framework, potentially leading to disputes over interpretation and enforcement. This directly contradicts the established legal context and creates a legal contradiction.
